instructions: This feeds the interactive menu page. Add or edit items here — no code changes needed. 'category' controls which color tab an item shows under. Each item's five text fields (tastingNotes, wineryProfile, farmingWinemaking, aboutCuvee, vintageDetails) are shown in full when a server clicks into the wine.

items.name: Cava, 'Reserva Brut'
items.tastingNotes: Ripe apple, lemon peel, white flowers, and toasted almond on the nose. Creamy palate with fine mousse, white peach, and saline minerality. Dry finish with a touch of brioche.
items.wineryProfile: 4th-generation Esteve family estate in Avinyonet del Penedès, farming since 1597. In 2022, earned the Elaborador Integral designation — Cava's equivalent of Champagne's Récoltant-Manipulant — held by only 14 producers in the D.O.
items.farmingWinemaking: All vineyards certified organic. Old vines (planted 1978–1988) at 250–275m on clay-calcareous soils. Hand-harvested at night, gently pressed, aged on the lees well beyond the legal minimum. Disgorged on demand.
items.aboutCuvee: Avinyó's flagship — vintage-dated, made from free-run juice only. Xarel·lo provides backbone and earthiness; Macabeo adds floral lift; Parellada brings acidity and elegance.
items.vintageDetails: 2022 was warm and dry in Penedès. High-elevation old vines and organic farming preserved freshness.

items.name: Champagne, 'Les 3 Terroirs' Blanc de Blancs Extra Brut
items.tastingNotes: Lemon, green apple, pear, and white peach on the nose, followed by hazelnut, toasted almond, and brioche from extended lees aging. Crisp and focused on the palate, with fine persistent bubbles, chalky minerality, and a clean, long finish.
items.wineryProfile: Founded in 1802 by Charles Gonet, the Maison is still family-owned — now managed by Sophie Gonet (7th generation). The estate owns 40+ hectares in the Côte des Blancs, including parcels in the Grand Cru villages of Avize, Oger, and Le Mesnil sur Oger, and into the hills of Sézanne and Vindey.
items.farmingWinemaking: Sustainable viticulture across 40 hectares of primarily Chardonnay. Extended aging on the lees gives the wines depth and textural complexity. Low dosage (2 g/L) keeps the profile precise and mineral.
items.aboutCuvee: A blend of Chardonnay from three distinct terroirs: Le Mesnil sur Oger (Grand Cru chalk, tension), Montgueux (cool, citrus-driven), and Vindey in the Sézannais (rounder, fleshier texture). The three sites together produce a complexity no single terroir could achieve.
items.vintageDetails: 2020 was a warm, generous Champagne vintage with strong ripeness balanced by enough acidity for structure. Extended élevage integrates toasty, nutty secondary notes and softens Chardonnay's natural acidity into a seamless, refined whole.

items.name: Champagne, 'Cuvée Rosé' Brut
items.grape: 100% Pinot Noir (10 Grand Cru crus, including Ambonnay, Bouzy, Louvois, and Tours-sur-Marne)
items.tastingNotes: Pomegranate, dark cherry, blood orange, and bergamot on the nose, with a hint of Sichuan pepper. Medium-to-full-bodied and vinous on the palate, with a frothy mousse and ripe, vibrant acidity. Long, perfumed finish.
items.wineryProfile: Founded in 1812, Laurent-Perrier reached its golden age under Bernard de Nonancourt starting in 1948. Built around the freshness of Chardonnay, the Maison is today one of the largest family-controlled Champagne houses, based in Tours-sur-Marne.
items.farmingWinemaking: Destemmed Pinot Noir grapes macerate on their skins for 48 to 72 hours to extract color and aroma, then the juice is drawn off and fermented separately — the saignée method. The non-vintage blend combines roughly equal parts base-year and reserve wine, resting 5 years on the lees.
items.aboutCuvee: Launched in 1968 by Bernard de Nonancourt, Cuvée Rosé pioneered the modern rosé Champagne category and remains the world's best-selling rosé Champagne. Sourced from 10 Grand Cru villages across the Montagne de Reims, including Bouzy, Ambonnay, Louvois, and Tours-sur-Marne.
items.vintageDetails: Non-vintage assemblage built on a roughly 50/50 base-year and reserve-wine blend. The current release, based on the 2019 vintage, shows pomegranate and dark cherry richness balanced by vibrant acidity.

items.name: Champagne, 'Ultra Brut' Brut Nature
items.grape: ~55% Chardonnay, 45% Pinot Noir (blend of 15 Grand and Premier Cru crus)
items.tastingNotes: Pale straw-yellow with a fine bead. Intense citrus and honeysuckle on the nose with a distinctive mineral edge. Balanced and sustained on the palate, with pear and grapefruit through to a fresh, lemony finish.
items.wineryProfile: Introduced in 1981 under Bernard de Nonancourt, Ultra Brut revived the House's 19th-century "Grand Vin Sans Sucre," a sugar-free style first crafted in 1889 for British clients. Laurent-Perrier remains one of the last houses producing true zero-dosage Champagne at this level.
items.farmingWinemaking: Only grapes from very ripe harvests with a naturally balanced sugar-to-acid ratio are selected, since no dosage corrects for imbalance at full dryness. The blend draws on 15 crus and rests at least 6 years on the lees, topped at disgorgement with reserve wine rather than dosage.
items.aboutCuvee: Ultra Brut is Champagne stripped to its essentials — no added sugar of any kind, revealing the intrinsic character of the Chardonnay-Pinot Noir blend and the chalk soils beneath it. A versatile, bone-dry style built for oysters and sushi.
items.vintageDetails: A non-vintage blend built only from exceptionally ripe years, since the sugar-acid balance needed without dosage depends on harvest quality. The result is a consistently dry, mineral house style unique to Laurent-Perrier.

items.name: Champagne, 'Grand Siècle No. 26' Brut
items.grape: 58% Chardonnay, 42% Pinot Noir · 100% Grand Cru (8 crus)
items.tastingNotes: White gold in color with fine, persistent bubbles. Honeysuckle, lemon, clementine, and fresh butter on the nose, with hazelnut and a touch of honey. Silky and mineral on the palate, with candied lemon and flaked almond through a long, precise finish.
items.wineryProfile: Grand Siècle was born from Bernard de Nonancourt's vision to recreate, through blending, a "perfect year" that no single vintage could deliver alone. Named for Louis XIV's Great Century and bottled in a replica 17th-century flask, it is Laurent-Perrier's prestige cuvée.
items.farmingWinemaking: Fruit is drawn exclusively from eight of Champagne's top-rated Grand Cru villages, including Le Mesnil-sur-Oger, Cramant, Avize, Oger, Ambonnay, Bouzy, and Verzy. Each vintage is vinified and aged separately before blending, then rests a further 10 years on the lees.
items.aboutCuvee: Now in its 26th release since 1959, Grand Siècle is never a single-vintage wine — three complementary vintage years are blended to "create" the ideal year nature alone rarely provides. One of the few prestige cuvées built around a Chardonnay-dominant blend.
items.vintageDetails: This iteration blends 65% 2012, 25% 2008, and 10% 2007 — three of the most celebrated Champagne vintages of the 21st century. Disgorged in 2023 after a decade on the lees, showing the generosity of 2012 tempered by 2008's structure.

items.name: Muscadet, 'La Grange' Sèvre-et-Maine
items.tastingNotes: Pale lemon with green reflections. Lime zest, green apple, and white peach, with crushed oyster shell, wet stone, and a light floral edge. Mineral-driven and textured on the palate — saline, round, with a dry, refreshing finish.
items.wineryProfile: 9th-generation family farming Muscadet since the late 17th century. Pierre-Marie and Marie Luneau have transformed the estate through certified biodynamics and a focus on single-parcel terroir bottlings. Widely considered one of the top producers in the appellation.
items.farmingWinemaking: Certified organic (Ecocert, 2013) and biodynamic (Demeter & Biodyvin, 2019). 27 hectares farmed without synthetic products. Whole-cluster pressing, ambient yeast fermentation, 6 months sur lie in underground concrete tanks lined with glass tile. No racking, no fining, no filtration. Minimal SO₂ at bottling.
items.aboutCuvee: From 35-year-old vines (planted 1974) on mica-schist and shale soils in Le Landreau. La Grange is the rounder, fleshier of the estate's two village wines — built on texture and depth rather than the firmer, more linear Le Verger.
items.vintageDetails: 2023 in Muscadet delivered freshness and purity — well-balanced acidity, ripe but precise. La Grange shows notable roundness and flesh in this vintage.

items.name: Sancerre, 'Les Coûtes'
items.tastingNotes: Dense and textured for Sancerre. Citrus zest, white peach, and gooseberry over crushed chalk and shell minerality. Full-bodied and precise on the palate — integrated mineral grip, phenolic structure, and a long, saline, mouthwatering finish.
items.wineryProfile: Based in the hamlet of Maimbray — a chalk-cliffed valley adjacent to Chavignol and Verdigny. Founded by brothers Pascal and Nicolas (Nicolas passed in 2005), now run by Pascal and his sons Victorien and Benjamin, who trained at Domaine Rousseau and Romanée-Conti respectively.
items.farmingWinemaking: Sustainable viticulture with restricted yields. Hand-harvested, each plot fermented separately in stainless steel. Lees aging through winter, spring bottling, unfiltered.
items.aboutCuvee: A single hillside plot of 50+ year-old vines on Kimmeridgian Marne — the same Terre Blanche bedrock as the great wines of Chavignol. Yields restricted to 45 hl/ha, stainless steel only, bottled unfiltered.
items.vintageDetails: 2025 brought freshness and clarity to Sancerre. Reverdy's old-vine Kimmeridgian fruit captures the year's tension and salinity well.

items.name: Albariño, 'Afelio'
items.region: Val do Salnés, Rías Baixas, Galicia, Spain
items.tastingNotes: Salt spray, white peach, lemon, and Atlantic air on the nose. Taut, saline, and finely cut on the palate, with vibrant citrus and stone fruit framed by granite-driven minerality and brisk acidity. Long, savory finish.
items.wineryProfile: Manuel "Chicho" Moldes launched his eponymous label in 2017 alongside his work as winemaker at Bodegas Fulcro. One of the leading figures of the new-wave Galician scene, focused on small-parcel, terroir-driven Albariños highlighting the contrast between schist and granite sites.
items.farmingWinemaking: Organic and biodynamic in practice (uncertified). Tiny parcels across the heart of Val do Salnés between Meaño and Cambados. Vines averaging 50+ years old on the traditional pergola system on granite posts. Native fermentation in stainless steel and old French oak (500–700L) at 21°C max.
items.aboutCuvee: A blend of up to 20 small parcels on granite bedrock — a counterpoint to Moldes's schist-based bottlings. The granite signature shows in the wine's tension, lift, and acidic spine.
items.vintageDetails: 2024 drinking with characteristic Salnés freshness and salinity.

items.name: Chenin Blanc, 'Les Argiles'
items.region: Vouvray (bottled in Montlouis), Loire Valley, France
items.tastingNotes: Bone-dry but textured — beeswax and fresh cream around honeydew, pear, and golden apple. Precise and high-intensity on the palate, with crushed rock, chalk, and lime zest cutting through a creamy mid-palate. Bracing acidity, long mineral finish.
items.wineryProfile: François Chidaine is widely regarded as the leading Chenin Blanc producer in Montlouis and Vouvray. He has grown the domaine from 4 hectares (inherited in 1989) to 45 hectares and holds the highest rating of any Loire producer from La Revue du Vin de France (one of only seven). Absent from the Vouvray AOC since 2014 — see below.
items.farmingWinemaking: Certified organic since 1992, biodynamic (Demeter) since 1999. Whole-cluster pressing, native yeast, no malolactic, 8–11 months on lees in 600L demi-muids, unfiltered, minimal sulfur.
items.aboutCuvee: 100% from clay-rich Vouvray plots around the legendary Clos Baudoin. 40-year-old bush vines on chalky clay over limestone. Because Chidaine's cellar is in Montlouis, the AOC has barred him from labeling these wines Vouvray since 2014 — they are sold as Vin de France.
items.vintageDetails: 2024 brought a return to Loire freshness after several warmer vintages. Chidaine's biodynamic farming produced wines of characteristic poise and mineral precision.

items.name: Chablis, '45°'
items.tastingNotes: Lemon zest, lime, crisp green apple, and white peach, with subtle orchard fruit, fresh butter, and mineral salinity. Taut and precise on the palate with chalky, crystalline acidity and a clean, long finish. Pure, stony Chablis built for aging.
items.wineryProfile: Clément Lavallée launched his own label in 2019 after working across Burgundy (Armand Heitz, Domaine des Terres de Velle), Château Margaux, Domaine Chapoutier, and Rutherglen, Australia. He now farms 14 hectares across Chablis, Côtes d'Auxerre, and Saint-Bris. His neighbors in Courgis include Thomas Pico (Pattes Loup) and Domaine De Moor.
items.farmingWinemaking: Organic in practice (uncertified) — no herbicides, no chemicals, gravity-flow cellar, native yeast, no sulfur during pressing. Aged 18 months on lees in stainless steel to preserve the site's mineral character without oxidative influence.
items.aboutCuvee: Named for the 45-degree gradient of the Kimmeridgian parcel from which it comes — the label is applied to the bottle at the same angle. From Courgis on the left bank of the Serein. Very limited production.
items.vintageDetails: 2023 in Chablis delivered freshness and balance — precise acidity and ripe but clean fruit. Lavallée's minimal-intervention approach lets the vintage's purity come through directly.

items.name: Rosé, 'Rubentis' Getariako Txakolina
items.grape: 50% Hondarribi Beltza, 50% Hondarribi Zuri (field blend, co-fermented)
items.tastingNotes: Pale pink with fuchsia tints and a fine, persistent natural spritz. Citrus zest, sour strawberry, wild berry, and herbal menthol on the nose. Light, zesty, and saline on the palate — tangy red fruit and crisp green apple lifted by a playful spritz. Low-alcohol, crisp, mineral finish.
items.wineryProfile: 5th-generation Ameztoi family has farmed cliffside vineyards above the Bay of Biscay in Getaria for over 200 years. They invented the rosé Txakoli style in the 2000s when most growers were removing their red Hondarrabi Beltza vines. Ameztoi preserved theirs — including a planting from 1840.
items.farmingWinemaking: Ecological farming on 20 hectares of sea-facing slopes between 50 and 200m elevation on limestone soils. Hand-harvested. White and red grapes destemmed and co-fermented together for 3 weeks in refrigerated stainless with native yeasts. Bottled with residual fermentation carbonation — never force-carbonated.
items.aboutCuvee: Rubentis was the first rosé Txakoli produced — inspired by historic claret-style wines once made on the Basque coast. The old Hondarribi Beltza vines (some from 1840) give the wine its color, structure, and tannic backbone.
items.vintageDetails: A young, just-released vintage drinking at full Atlantic-fresh, spritzy expression.

items.name: 'Le Rosé' Vaucluse
items.region: Dentelles de Montmirail, Vaucluse, Rhône, France
items.tastingNotes: Pale salmon-peach. Wild strawberry, raspberry, and cherry on the nose, with lemon peel, jasmine, and crushed stone. Creamy and full-bodied for a rosé — good salinity, citrus core, and a long, mineral finish. Decanter compared it to Domaine Tempier Bandol.
items.wineryProfile: Chêne Bleu sits at the four-corner intersection of Gigondas, Côtes du Ventoux, Côtes du Rhône, and Séguret in the Dentelles de Montmirail. The Rolet family acquired the abandoned medieval estate (a former Knights Templar priory) in 1994 and opted out of the AOC system entirely to pursue full quality freedom.
items.farmingWinemaking: Certified organic and biodynamic at 530–630m elevation — the highest vineyard in the southern Rhône. Cool-climate altitude with clay, limestone, and stone soils. Hand-harvested, double-sorted. Direct press for the rosé, slow cool fermentation, 25% in barrel for texture.
items.aboutCuvee: A food rosé built for ageability. High altitude preserves the acidity and structure most southern French rosés lack. The Mourvèdre and Syrah components provide the Bandol-like backbone.
items.vintageDetails: 2024 delivered good freshness in the southern Rhône — cooler nights at altitude preserved acidity and aromatic precision.

items.name: Garnacha, 'El Mentridano'
items.tastingNotes: Pale ruby. Floral aromatics of dark cherry, wild strawberry, dried herbs, and violet. Light-footed on the palate with delicate tannins, mineral lift, and a fresh, savory finish. More Burgundian in weight and feel than Spanish.
items.wineryProfile: Vitícola Mentridana is the continuation of the Méntrida vineyards of Daniel Landi (Comando G) — handed in 2021 to childhood friend Curro Barreño. Curro previously worked at Fedellos do Couto in Galicia making light, precise reds. A Gredos native, he's returned to build on Landi's foundation.
items.farmingWinemaking: Old-vine Garnacha at 600m on sandy granitic soils. Organic farming, hand-harvested, whole-cluster fermented in open-top oak vats with native yeasts. Short maceration for gentle extraction. 7 months in large neutral French oak foudres. No filtration, no stabilization.
items.aboutCuvee: The village-level wine — Curro's family Garnacha vines around Méntrida. More accessible and drinkable than the higher-altitude Las Uvas de la Ira (850m) and single-vineyard Cantos del Diablo.
items.vintageDetails: 2022 was warmer in Gredos. Short macerations and whole-cluster work kept the wine light, floral, and fresh despite the heat.

items.name: Pinot Noir, 'Les Montroziers' Nuits-Saint-Georges
items.tastingNotes: Intense garnet. Raspberry, black cherry, plum, and peony on the nose, with forest floor, sweet spice, and graphite. Smooth and perfumed on the palate — velvety tannins, lively acidity, medium-long finish. More Vosne-Romanée in character than typical Nuits.
items.wineryProfile: Founded in 1825, Domaine Faiveley is one of Burgundy's largest family domaines — 7th generation under Erwan Faiveley and his sister Eve. They own 120 hectares including 12 Grands Crus and 25 Premiers Crus across the Côte d'Or and Côte Chalonnaise.
items.farmingWinemaking: The 4.84-hectare Montroziers parcels are farmed organically (uncertified). Vine ages range from 1928 plantings to more recent additions on east-facing clay-limestone soils. Erwan Faiveley has moved toward more precision and freshness since 2005. 14 months in fine-grained French oak (20% new), then 2 months tank rest before bottling.
items.aboutCuvee: Named for Maurice Colrat de Montrozier, witness to the 1945 Faiveley family marriage. Previously two separate parcellaire bottlings (Aux Lavières and Les Argillats), now blended into one village cuvée — the fruit leaning toward the Vosne-Romanée side of Nuits.
items.vintageDetails: 2022 was a strong Burgundy vintage — generous and ripe, with acidity preserved by cooler nights. Montroziers shows the vintage's balance and purity. Supple now with a decant, but built for 5–10 years.

items.name: Châteauneuf-du-Pape, 'Ancien Domaine de Pontifès'
items.tastingNotes: Deep Châteauneuf garnet. Black cherry, blackberry, garrigue (thyme, rosemary, lavender), warm earth, black pepper, and licorice on the nose. Powerful and balanced on the palate — concentrated fruit, structured tannins, ripe mid-palate, long savory finish.
items.wineryProfile: 5th-generation family domaine producing wine in the southern Rhône since 1895, across Châteauneuf-du-Pape, Lirac, and Côtes du Rhône. 4 hectares within Châteauneuf and 16 hectares in Roquemaure. The name "Ancien Domaine des Pontifes" references the property's historic ties to the Avignon papacy.
items.farmingWinemaking: Certified organic since 2012 (Ecocert) — no chemical inputs, limited mechanical soil work, minimal sulfites. Cellar in Roquemaure is built into a stone cliff (L'Escatillon), giving exceptional thermal stability year-round.
items.aboutCuvee: The estate's flagship Châteauneuf-du-Pape — old-vine Grenache supported by Mourvèdre. Classically structured rather than modern in style.
items.vintageDetails: 2023 in the southern Rhône produced wines with good freshness alongside typical ripeness — a balanced vintage drinking with notable elegance for Châteauneuf.

items.name: Bordeaux
items.tastingNotes: Deep garnet with a brick-orange rim. Dried plum, blackcurrant, cedar, tobacco, leather, and forest floor on the nose. Silky and balanced on the palate with softened tannins, dried fruit, a spiced finish, and lingering mineral edge. Fully mature right-bank Bordeaux.
items.wineryProfile: Founded in the early 1980s by Régis Moro, who farmed organically from the outset and used low-intervention cellar work at a time when Bordeaux was moving in the opposite direction. The estate sits in Castillon-Côtes de Bordeaux, a few kilometers from Château Le Puy. The two estates shared a philosophy and a long friendship — the Amoreau (Le Puy) family eventually acquired Champs de Mars, preserving Moro's approach under new ownership.
items.farmingWinemaking: Certified organic from the start, biodynamic since 2013. 20 hectares on clay-limestone soils. Spontaneous fermentations, no temperature control, no new oak, gravity-flow movement. Bottled without fining or filtration.
items.aboutCuvee: The estate bottling — 80/20 Merlot and Cabernet Franc from sandy clay-limestone terroir in Castillon. At 23 years of age the wine is at a fully mature drinking window, showing the complexity of great farming and patient aging.
items.vintageDetails: 2003 was the heat-wave vintage across France. On the right bank, Merlot handled the extreme heat well, producing concentrated, plush wines. After more than two decades, Champs de Mars 2003 is fully integrated — fruit dried and concentrated into complex secondary flavors with silky tannins.

items.name: Tempranillo, 'Senda del Oro'
items.region: Ribera del Duero, Castilla y León, Spain
items.tastingNotes: Deep ruby-purple. Blackberry, raspberry, and dark cherry with violets, incense, and a floral pastille note that grows spicier with air. Juicy and full-bodied on the palate — concentrated dark fruit, balsamic note, soft tannins, and a fresh acidic core. Modern, restrained Ribera.
items.wineryProfile: Second-generation family estate in Mambrilla de Castrejón, Burgos subzone — the highest and coolest part of Ribera del Duero. Founded by Juan Carlos Vizcarra in the late 1980s, now run with his daughter Celia. One of the few estates in the region vinifying only estate-grown fruit.
items.farmingWinemaking: 35+ hectares of estate vines, some over 80 years old, at 800–850m on limestone, clay, and gravel. Hand-harvested, double-sorted, gravity-flow (no pumps). Stainless steel fermentation, malolactic in barrel, 6–7 months in used French and American oak.
items.aboutCuvee: The entry-level cuvée — from the youngest plots (~15 years old) with emphasis on pure fruit over oak influence. The 2024 harvest timing and macerations were adjusted for the warm vintage to maintain freshness.
items.vintageDetails: 2024 was warm in Ribera. Juan Carlos and Celia picked earlier and used shorter macerations to preserve fruit and acidity — the new-wave Ribera approach at its most deliberate.

items.name: Rivesaltes Ambré
items.tastingNotes: Deep amber and oxidative — dried fig, date, toffee, walnut, and caramel, with orange peel and tobacco on a long, warming finish.
items.wineryProfile: Run by Jean-Hubert and Brigitte Verdaguer, Domaine de Rancy is one of only a handful of estates still producing traditional Ambré-style Rivesaltes in the Roussillon.
items.farmingWinemaking: Organically farmed old vines. Unlike most wines, oxidation is the point here, not a flaw: barrels sit in ambient (not heated) cellar conditions with an intentional air gap, so the wine slowly "maderizes" over decades — much like a fine oxidative sherry — building nutty, dried-fruit, and rancio complexity while its natural acidity keeps it from turning flat or flabby.
items.aboutCuvee: A Vin Doux Naturel — fortified with neutral grape spirit during fermentation to halt sugar conversion and preserve natural sweetness. This 2000 vintage rested 21 years before bottling in August 2021.
items.vintageDetails: The 2000 vintage is considered one of the estate's standout recent releases.

items.name: Sauternes
items.tastingNotes: Honeysuckle, tropical fruit, and apricot aromas; rich and layered with marmalade and toasted brioche, finishing long and sweet but beautifully balanced.
items.wineryProfile: Founded by Carmelite monks in the 18th century, Rieussec has been owned by Domaines Baron de Rothschild (Lafite) since 1984 — one of Sauternes's largest and most prestigious estates, second in stature only to Château d'Yquem.
items.farmingWinemaking: 93 hectares of gravelly, clay-rich soil. Botrytis cinerea (noble rot) develops unevenly across the vineyard depending on morning fog off the Ciron river, so pickers return repeatedly over weeks, selecting only individual berries at peak shrivel — in poor years, the entire crop can be declassified rather than bottled under the Rieussec name.
items.aboutCuvee: The Grand Vin of Rieussec. 2009 was hailed as an outstanding vintage across Sauternes, among the finest in the estate's modern history.
items.vintageDetails: 2009 is considered one of Rieussec's finest vintages, balancing rich botrytis character with vibrant, food-friendly acidity.

items.name: Sauternes
items.tastingNotes: Red-gold color; roasted chestnut, autumn leaves, and dark bitter honey, with tarte tatin, baked custard, aniseed, and a very long, spice-laced finish.
items.wineryProfile: The undisputed First Growth of Sauternes, farmed continuously since 1593; acquired by LVMH in 1999 — the same year as this vintage.
items.farmingWinemaking: 100 hectares under vine. Pickers walk the rows repeatedly over weeks, harvesting only berries that have individually reached the ideal stage of noble rot — sometimes grape by grape rather than by bunch. The selection is so strict that in vintages where botrytis and ripeness don't align (as in 1992, 2012, and several others), no Grand Vin is produced at all and the crop is sold off anonymously.
items.aboutCuvee: One of the world's most collectible white wines — Yquem holds the record for the most expensive bottle of white wine ever sold at auction (an 1811 vintage, $117,000).
items.vintageDetails: The 1999 shows beautifully 25 years on, with decades of life still ahead.

items.name: Pineau des Charentes, 'Très Vieux'
items.tastingNotes: Deep amber; dried nuts, walnut, raisin, and candied orange, with rancio complexity, a silky texture, and a long, warming finish.
items.wineryProfile: The Lhéraud family has farmed the same Charente vineyards since 1680; Guy Lhéraud began bottling Cognac and Pineau under his own name in 1970.
items.farmingWinemaking: This is a mistelle, not a fermented wine in the traditional sense: grape must never begins converting sugar to alcohol because Cognac is added immediately, preserving the fruit's full natural sweetness. Decades of oak aging then build the dried-fruit, nut, and rancio complexity that a young Pineau simply can't have. "Très Vieux" denotes a legal minimum of 10 years — this bottling has rested more than six times that.
items.aboutCuvee: A rare 1962 vintage bottling — one of the oldest expressions of the Charente region's signature vin de liqueur.
items.vintageDetails: 1962 fruit, aged over 60 years, showing the deep rancio and dried-fruit complexity only extreme age can produce.

items.name: 30 Year Tawny Port
items.grape: Touriga Nacional, Touriga Franca, Tinta Roriz
items.tastingNotes: Amber with green hues; intense dried fruit, vanilla, and honey on the nose; harmonious, concentrated, and unctuous on the palate with a long finish.
items.wineryProfile: Founded in 1638 by German diplomat Cristiano Kopke, the House is recognized as the oldest Port company in the world, sourcing fruit from its historic Quinta de São Luiz estate.
items.farmingWinemaking: Hand-picked grapes are destemmed, crushed, and fermented with constant pumping-over to extract color and tannin, until fortification is added at the desired sweetness (baumé). Unlike Vintage Port, which matures in bottle, Tawny ages in relatively small oak casks that let in steady, controlled oxygen — over decades this softens the tannins and turns the wine amber-brown, developing dried-fruit, nut, and caramel notes in place of Vintage Port's dark fruit and structure.
items.aboutCuvee: An aged Tawny built from Kopke's deep cellar reserves — the average-age labeling system allows blending across decades for a style with no single-vintage identity.
items.vintageDetails: A non-vintage blend; the "30 Years Old" designation reflects an official, audited average-age certification.

items.name: Vintage Port
items.grape: Touriga Nacional, Touriga Franca, Tinta Roriz, Tinto Cão
items.tastingNotes: Opaque purple-ruby; peppery, licorice-scented raspberry and blackcurrant fruit, with dark chocolate, cedar, and violet — full-bodied with firm, well-integrated tannins.
items.wineryProfile: Warre's, founded in 1670, is the oldest British Port house, known for a drier, more structured house style; now part of the Symington family portfolio.
items.farmingWinemaking: Fruit sourced from top Douro quintas including Quinta da Cavadinha. Because this is a declared Vintage Port — only produced in the best years, unlike Tawny's yearly blending — nearly all of its aging and development happens sealed in the bottle over decades, throwing heavy sediment along the way and requiring careful decanting before serving.
items.aboutCuvee: 1994 was a widely and enthusiastically "declared" vintage across the Douro — Wine Spectator called it one of the best of the 20th century.
items.vintageDetails: Now over 30 years old and entering a long peak drinking window.

items.name: Boston Bual
items.grape: 85% Bual, 15% very old Tinta Negra Mole
items.tastingNotes: Light amber-tawny; dried fig, walnut, maple syrup, and mahogany, with cinnamon-clove spice and citrus peel; long, dry finish balanced by vibrant acidity.
items.wineryProfile: A collaboration between importer The Rare Wine Co. and Barbeito's Ricardo Freitas, named for Boston's historic 18th-century love of sweeter Madeiras.
items.farmingWinemaking: 85% of the blend is Bual aged 10–20 years under canteiro; the remaining 15% is very old Tinta Negra Mole (40–60 years old), added specifically to lend the depth and complexity of a true vintage Madeira to what is otherwise a non-vintage bottling — a blending trick unique to Barbeito's approach.
items.aboutCuvee: Part of The Rare Wine Co.'s Historic Series, each wine named for an American port city — Madeira was America's most fashionable wine in the 18th and 19th centuries.
items.vintageDetails: A non-vintage blend, widely praised by critics as a benchmark, richer-style Madeira.

items.name: Sercial Reserva Velha
items.tastingNotes: Pale amber-gold; classic Sercial profile of nuts, orange peel, and mineral salinity, with bracing, palate-cleansing acidity and an extremely long, dry finish.
items.wineryProfile: Vinhos Barbeito, founded by Mário de Vasconcelos and still family-run, is celebrated for pure, unadulterated Madeiras made without caramel coloring — called "the established star" of the island by Jancis Robinson.
items.farmingWinemaking: Sercial is grown at Madeira's highest elevations specifically for maximum acidity, since it's the driest and most age-worthy of the island's classic grapes. Barbeito is also known for never adding caramel coloring, unlike most Madeira houses — meaning the wine's deep amber-gold hue here comes entirely from over a century of real oxidative aging, not additives.
items.aboutCuvee: An 1898 vintage is exceptionally rare — one of the oldest wines on the list, offered as a rare 1oz pour given its scarcity and value.
items.vintageDetails: At over 125 years of age, this is a museum-piece Madeira, capable of near-indefinite aging once opened.

items.name: Bual Solera
items.tastingNotes: Deep mahogany with golden highlights; dried fig, date, raisin, candied citrus, caramel, and toffee, with clove and cinnamon spice and a long, smoky-oak finish.
items.wineryProfile: Cossart Gordon, founded in 1745 by Scottish brothers Francis and Thomas Newton, is the oldest company in the Madeira trade; today part of the Madeira Wine Company alongside Blandy's.
items.farmingWinemaking: The 1845 Bual was converted into a solera in 1875 to help cope with the wine shortage caused by the Phylloxera epidemic, which had devastated Madeira's vineyards. Because a solera continually refreshes itself with younger wine, it can theoretically run forever — this one has been kept alive for approximately 150 years and became Cossart Gordon's first "Centenary" bottling, marking the house's 1745 founding.
items.aboutCuvee: A true solera — meaning only a portion of any given bottle is actually from 1845, with wine continually added and refreshed over generations, yet the character of the founding vintage carries through.
items.vintageDetails: A rare opportunity to taste a continuously-running 19th-century solera.

items.name: Malvasia Reserva Velha
items.tastingNotes: Refulgent amber; marmalade, lanolin, toffee apple, dried herbs, and burnt toast, with walnut, lemongrass, ginger, and white pepper through a beautifully balanced, deeply persistent finish.
items.wineryProfile: Vinhos Barbeito, still run by the founding de Vasconcelos family, sourced this bottle from the historic Acciaioly cellar tranche — one of the rarest surviving lots of 19th-century Madeira.
items.farmingWinemaking: Malvasia (Malmsey) is the richest and sweetest of Madeira's four classic grapes, traditionally grown on trellises closer to sea level than drier varieties like Sercial. This bottle's cask spent nearly two centuries in the lodge before bottling, developing extraordinary depth while Madeira's signature acidity — preserved by the island's slow oxidative aging process — keeps the wine lively rather than cloying.
items.aboutCuvee: At nearly two centuries old, this is very likely the oldest wine on the list — a true museum pour, offered by the ounce given its rarity and value.
items.vintageDetails: The 1834 vintage; recent tasting notes describe a wine still "in full bloom," with decades of life remaining despite its age.
